title: US tightens chip export rules as Taiwan's TSMC expands Arizona fabs
author: Daria Litvinova
body:
TAIPEI — The Biden administration's successor team announced expanded export controls on advanced semiconductors to China on Friday, as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. held a groundbreaking ceremony for its third fabrication plant in Arizona.

Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo said the rules target equipment that can produce chips below 7 nanometers, citing national security risks tied to artificial intelligence and military systems. NVIDIA and AMD said they would work with the Commerce Department to ensure compliance while maintaining supplies to allied customers in Japan, South Korea and the Netherlands.

TSMC chairman Mark Liu told investors that the company would continue expanding in Hsinchu and Tainan while accelerating its U.S. investments in Phoenix. Apple CEO Tim Cook, visiting Taipei, praised TSMC as a critical partner for iPhone and Mac production. Liu warned that geopolitical tension across the Taiwan Strait remained the biggest uncertainty for global technology supply chains.

Chinese commerce ministry spokesperson He Yadong condemned the controls as an abuse of trade policy and vowed countermeasures to protect firms such as Huawei and SMIC. President Xi Jinping told officials in Beijing that China must achieve self-reliance in chip design, lithography tools and advanced packaging.

Taiwanese President Lai Ching-te said the island's democracy and semiconductor industry were inseparable, urging partners in Washington, Tokyo and Canberra to support deterrence in the Indo-Pacific. Japan's Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba announced new subsidies for Rapidus and Tokyo Electron, while Dutch firm ASML confirmed it would seek licenses before shipping extreme ultraviolet scanners.

U.S. House Speaker Mike Johnson met bipartisan lawmakers in Washington to discuss a Taiwan security assistance package. Pentagon officials said any blockade of the Taiwan Strait would disrupt shipments from the Port of Kaohsiung and raise insurance costs across the South China Sea.

Analysts at Morgan Stanley estimated that full enforcement of the rules could delay China's domestic production of high-end GPUs by at least three years. TSMC shares rose in Taipei trading after the company raised its 2026 capital spending forecast, while suppliers including ASE Technology and UMC outlined plans for new testing facilities in Singapore and Malaysia.
